- Outpatient hospital services
- Outpatient medical and surgical services and supplies
- Pap tests and pelvic exams
- Physical exams
- Physical therapy
- Pneumococcal shots
- Prescription drugs (such as injections received at a medical office, but not medications you can take on your own)
- Prostate cancer screenings
- Prosthetic items
- Pulmonary rehabilitation
- Rural health clinic services
- Second opinions regarding non-emergency surgery (including a third opinion if two physicians don't agree)
- Smoking cessation counseling
- Speech-language pathology services
- Surgical dressing services (for wounds)
- Special electronic communications with medical professionals (in some rural areas)
- Tests (including x-rays, MRIs, and CT scans)
- Treatment and medication related to organ transplants
- Emergency care in foreign hospitals (but only near a U.S. border)

Despite the length of that list, there are many exceptions and limits to care under Part B. Some will be addressed in the next few sections. Others are beyond the scope of this course.

If you're automatically enrolled in Part B, your benefits will start at the same time as Part A. They'll continue until you stop paying the monthly premium or you contact Social Security to opt out. If you stop paying the premium, benefits will end after a 90-day grace period, which begins on the first day of the month after a bill is sent out. If you opt out by contacting Social Security, they'll stop on the last day of the month following the month of your cancellation. (Canceling on April 15, for example, would result in benefits stopping after May 31.)

data.html:
Medicare Part B: Office Visits

Part B will pay 80% of Medicare-approved charges for doctor visits. The patient or other insurance will need to pay the remaining 20%, as well as the annual deductible.

data.pageTitle: Medicare Part B: Charges and Assignment
data.html:
Medicare Part B: Charges and Assignment

Health care providers who treat Part B enrollees need to follow certain rules. The exact rules to follow will depend on whether a provider is a "participating provider," a "non-participating provider" or an "opt-out provider." These terms tend to confuse people. So if you're in a position to help a senior with insurance questions, you will want to read the rest of this section very carefully.

Participating providers in Medicare agree to accept "assignment" whenever they provide services under Part B. Assignment refers to the amount of money that Medicare believes should be charged for a particular service and how a Medicare claim is made. When doctors accept assignment, Medicare directly pays them 80% of the government's assigned charge and makes the patient responsible for the remaining 20%. Participating providers cannot charge Medicare patients more than the government's assigned charge. However, they are still allowed to set their own fees for services that aren't covered by the program.

Non-participating providers can choose whether to accept assignment on a case-by-case basis. When they accept assignment, Medicare directly pays for 80% of the assigned charge, and the patient pays the remaining 20%. The assigned charge for a non-participating provider will be slightly smaller than for a participating provider. When they accept assignment, non-participating providers are not allowed to charge more than the assigned amount.

When non-participating providers decide not to accept assignment, they can charge more for services than Medicare's assigned amount. The patient will need to pay the difference between the provider's actual charge and Medicare's assigned charge, but the actual charge can be no more than 115% of the assigned charge. In addition to paying the difference between the provider's actual charge and Medicare's assigned charge, patients will also be responsible for 20% of Medicare's assigned charge.

Regardless of how much they actually owe, patients who see non-participating doctors who don't accept assignment might have to pay providers personally for their care and then file a claim afterwards with Medicare for reimbursement. In other words, Medicare might not pay the provider directly.

data.html:
Example: Non-Participating Providers

Since the rules regarding non-participating providers can be confusing, let's consider an example. Imagine that Medicare has assigned a $100 charge for a medical procedure from a non-participating provider and that the provider has refused to accept assignment. The provider would be allowed to charge up to $115 for the procedure, and the expenses would be handled as follows:

  • $80 would be paid by Medicare, since Medicare pays 80% of the assigned charge. (The patient might need to pay this amount directly to the provider first and then seek reimbursement from Medicare.)

  • $20 would be paid by the patient as coinsurance, since Part B enrollees are responsible for 20% of assigned charges.

  • $15 would be paid by the patient as an additional out-of-pocket expense because the non-participating provider declined assignment.

It's very important not to confuse non-participating providers with opt-out providers. Opt-out providers have signed an agreement stating they will not accept Medicare as payment for services. If a Medicare beneficiary is treated by an opt-out provider, Medicare will usually not pay for any of the treatment. In general, opt-out providers will only be compensated by Medicare if they treat someone in an emergency situation. All other charges from an opt-out provider will need to be paid by the patient or by other insurance.

A provider has one chance each year to enroll as a participating provider or a non-participating provider. Opt-out providers need to wait at least two years before re-enrolling as participating providers or non-participating providers. Some types of medical professionals are required to be participating providers. Others are allowed to be non-participating providers but are not allowed to be opt-out providers.

Most physicians are either participating providers or non-participating providers and, therefore, accept payments from Medicare. But simply asking doctors if they "accept" Medicare won't guarantee that the patient will have the lowest-possible out-of-pocket expenses. The more specific question to ask is, "Do you accept assignment?" Medicare's online directory allows patients to search for local providers who always accept assignment.

data.html:
Medicare Part B: Preventive Care

In most cases, Medicare will only pay for treatment that's considered medically necessary. However, under Part B, care that is not exactly necessary to treat an illness or injury can still be covered if it's considered "preventive care." Preventive care is care that can either reasonably detect a medical problem before symptoms are noticed or reasonably stop a medical problem from occurring. Examples of preventive care are vaccines, flu shots, cancer screenings and routine physical exams.

Changes to the U.S. health care system in 2011 via the Affordable Care Act resulted in more coverage of preventive services in Medicare. Under the old system, beneficiaries were responsible for 20% of the assigned charge for preventive care, and they were entitled to just one preventive physical exam (which was only covered during a person's first year in Medicare). As a result of the Affordable Care Act, seniors are no longer required to pay for any of the assigned charge for preventive care (including deductibles), and they can receive routine physicals every year.

In spite of the reforms, certain restrictions can exist for coverage of preventive care. Some of the most common restrictions are:

  • The prescriber or medical provider must accept assignment

  • Full coverage might not apply if a preventive service is utilized too frequently

data.html:
Medicare Part B: Outpatient Care

Part B doesn't just cover services received at doctors' offices. It also pays for medical treatment that a person receives as an outpatient at the hospital. Here are some hospital-related scenarios that would be covered under Part B instead of Part A:

  • A patient goes to the emergency room but is never officially admitted to the facility

  • A patient has outpatient surgery at a hospital

  • A patient goes into the hospital for a blood transfusion or for medication that can't be self-administered

Outpatient services are different from other services covered under Part B because the patient usually pays a copayment for them instead of a coinsurance fee. In other words, the patient pays a specific dollar amount rather than a specific percentage of Medicare's assigned charge. The size of the copayment is different for every kind of outpatient service, but no copayment for a single service can be more than the Medicare Part A deductible.

In some situations, patients receiving outpatient care might be responsible for coinsurance fees as well as copayments. For example, a patient might pay a specific dollar amount for each trip to the emergency room. Then the patient might also be billed separately for doctors' services performed during that visit. Instead of paying a flat fee for those doctors' services, the patient might be responsible for at least 20% of the charge.

data.html:
Medicare Part B: Medical Equipment

Medicare Part B will pay for 80% of the assigned cost for "durable medical equipment." Durable medical equipment is prescribed, medically necessary equipment that's designed for long-term home use. It usually cannot be something that would help someone without a medical condition. If the equipment is used while a patient is receiving hospice care or home health care under Medicare Part A, it will be covered under Part A instead of by Part B.

Durable Medical Equipment

Common examples of durable medical equipment include the following items:

  • Wheelchairs

  • Canes

  • Crutches

  • Dialysis machines

  • Walkers

  • Oxygen tanks

  • Special beds

Some items, such as eyeglasses and hearing aids, might seem to fit into the definition of "durable medical equipment" but are usually not covered by Medicare. These excluded items are sometimes covered by private insurance companies that are part of the Medicare Advantage system. (You'll read more about Medicare Advantage shortly.)

Just as they should when looking for a physician, Medicare beneficiaries should ask medical supply companies if they accept assignment. If a supplier doesn't accept assignment, the patient may be required to pay more than 20% of the equipment's assigned charge. If the supplier is not part of the Medicare system at all, Medicare will pay for none of the equipment.

data.pageTitle: Medicare Part B: Enrollment Periods and Late Fees
data.html:
Medicare Part B: Enrollment Periods and Late Fees

Although Part B is optional, many people will be enrolled in it automatically. In most cases, Part B enrollment will be automatic for 65-year-olds who are already receiving Social Security and for people who've qualified for Medicare Part A as a result of a disability. If they don't want Part B, they must follow the instructions contained on their Medicare card. Otherwise, their monthly Part B premiums will be deducted from their Social Security payments.

Other groups of potential beneficiaries will only be covered by Part B if they actively enroll in it. This rule applies to people who are 65 or older but not receiving Social Security, people who have end-stage renal disease, and people who live in Puerto Rico. Also, while they are not required to join Medicare, anyone who wants Medicare Part A and is required to pay a premium for it must also enroll in Part B.

Individuals who aren't automatically enrolled in Part B can only opt into the program during specific enrollment periods. For 65-year-olds who are not receiving Social Security benefits, their initial enrollment period starts three months prior to the month of their 65th birthday and extends to three months after their birth month. Someone who was born on September 15, for example, would have an initial open enrollment period from June 1 through December 31 of the year they turn 65.
data.markdown:
# Medicare Part B: Enrollment Periods and Late Fees
Although Part B is optional, many people will be enrolled in it automatically. In most cases, Part B enrollment will be automatic for 65-year-olds who are already receiving Social Security and for people who've qualified for Medicare Part A as a result of a disability. If they don't want Part B, they must follow the instructions contained on their Medicare card. Otherwise, their monthly Part B premiums will be deducted from their Social Security payments.

Other groups of potential beneficiaries will only be covered by Part B if they actively enroll in it. This rule applies to people who are 65 or older but not receiving Social Security, people who have end-stage renal disease, and people who live in Puerto Rico. Also, while they are not required to join Medicare, anyone who wants Medicare Part A and is required to pay a premium for it must also enroll in Part B.

Individuals who aren't automatically enrolled in Part B can only opt into the program during specific enrollment periods. For 65-year-olds who are not receiving Social Security benefits, their initial enrollment period starts three months prior to the month of their 65th birthday and extends to three months after their birth month. Someone who was born on September 15, for example, would have an initial open enrollment period from June 1 through December 31 of the year they turn 65.

data.html:
When Coverage Begins

The day when Part B coverage begins for a 65-year-old will depend on when the enrollment actually occurs. Here are the potential starting dates for someone whose birthday is on September 15:

  • If enrollment occurs within the three months prior to the birth month (June through August), coverage would begin on the first day of the birth month (September 1)

  • If enrollment occurs during the birth month (September), coverage would begin on the first day of the following month (October 1)

  • If enrollment occurs during the three months after the birth month (October, November, or December), coverage would begin on the first day of the month following their enrollment

Seniors who let their initial enrollment period pass without enrolling in Part B won't get another chance to enroll until an annual open enrollment period. The annual enrolment period lasts from January 1 through March 31. This enrollment period is also open to disabled people who still qualify for Medicare but declined Part B when it was first offered to them.
data.markdown:
# When Coverage Begins
The day when Part B coverage begins for a 65-year-old will depend on when the enrollment actually occurs. Here are the potential starting dates for someone whose birthday is on September 15:

- If enrollment occurs within the three months prior to the birth month (June through August), coverage would begin on the first day of the birth month (September 1)
- If enrollment occurs during the birth month (September), coverage would begin on the first day of the following month (October 1)
- If enrollment occurs during the three months after the birth month (October, November, or December), coverage would begin on the first day of the month following their enrollment

Seniors who let their initial enrollment period pass without enrolling in Part B won't get another chance to enroll until an annual open enrollment period. The annual enrolment period lasts from January 1 through March 31. This enrollment period is also open to disabled people who still qualify for Medicare but declined Part B when it was first offered to them.

data.html:
Penalties for Late Enrollment

Since Part B requires beneficiaries to pay monthly premiums, some people choose to go without it. Anyone who is considering not enrolling in Part B should keep in mind that they may face some financial penalties at a later date. The penalties are designed to encourage people to enroll in Part B as soon as possible instead of waiting until they're sick.

If someone declines Part B and later decides to enroll, the person will often be forced to pay a higher monthly premium. A 10% penalty will be added for every 12-month period that the eligible person went without Part B. For seniors who decline Part B and enroll later in life, the penalty lasts as long as they're in Medicare. For people under 65, it lasts until their 65th birthday. The penalty is waived in situations where a person went without coverage for less than a full 12-month period.

If You Have Insurance Through Your Employer

People who decide against retiring at age 65 are probably glad there's a big exception regarding Part B penalties: The 10% penalty will be waived for people who decline Part B if they're already covered by group health insurance through current employment. This waiver also applies to people under 65 with disabilities, but not to people with end-stage renal disease.

People who decline Part B because they already have group coverage through their employer or a family member's employer can change their mind and enroll in Part B at any time while they're still covered by the group plan. In these cases, coverage will often begin on the first day of the month of enrollment.

When their group coverage or the employment related to the group coverage ends, people will have a limited amount of time to enroll in Part B without facing the 10% penalty. The period to enroll in Part B without paying higher premiums lasts eight months and begins the month after either group coverage ended or the employment related to the group coverage ended, whichever is earlier. According to the Social Security Administration, the eight-month rule might not apply to someone whose employment or group coverage ends during the person's initial enrollment period (the seven-month period around the person's 65th birthday).

In general, the exceptions related to late penalties for Part A (explained earlier) also apply to Part B.

data.html:
COBRA Rules

Many Medicare-eligible Americans who remain employed are misinformed about the relationship between Part B's late enrollment rules and COBRA coverage. Under federal COBRA rules, former employees are allowed to remain insured by their former employer's group plan for several months if they agree to pay for the insurance themselves. Although COBRA ensures that many unemployed people can remain insured, it doesn't stop the eight-month clock for enrolling in penalty-free Part B. The clock only stops or becomes a non-issue when the person is insured through current employment or the current employment of a family member.

The starting date for coverage that's obtained during the eight months after losing employment or losing other group coverage will depend on when Medicare enrollment actually occurs. If enrollment occurs during the first month, Part B will start on the first day of that month. If enrollment occurs during any of the seven remaining months, coverage will start on the first day of the month after enrollment. For example, if someone is insured by an employer's group plan, is laid off in January and enrolls in Part B during that same month, coverage would begin retroactively on January 1. If the same person were to wait to enroll until March, insurance under Part B would start on April 1.

Cancelling Part B

People who already have Part B can cancel it at any time. When they do, their insurance will stop at the end of the month after the month in which they contacted Medicare. Medicare can cancel someone's Part B insurance if a beneficiary goes three months without paying the required premiums. Grace periods beyond 90 days can be granted in special circumstances, such as when the beneficiary is temporarily incapacitated.

Anyone who is thinking of canceling or declining Part B because they have coverage through another group plan should discuss the matter with their employer or their company's HR department before making a decision. Companies employing fewer than 20 workers sometimes provide fewer benefits to plan members who are eligible for Medicare. The section "Group Health Insurance" contains more information about this issue.

data.html:
Medicare Part C (Medicare Advantage)

Up until now, the information in this level has pertained to what we can call "original Medicare," which is administered by the federal government. At the time this material was being written, roughly half of seniors in the Medicare system were enrolled in original Medicare and had the coverage we've previously described.

The remaining half of Medicare's seniors were enrolled in "Medicare Advantage." Medicare Advantage is similar to original Medicare but is administered by private insurance companies that receive federal funding. This semi-privatized version of Medicare is sometimes known as "Medicare Part C" and was preceded by a similar program called "Medicare+Choice."

Like an HMO or PPO

Medicare Advantage was created under the assumption that paying private insurers to administer Medicare benefits would save the government money and improve services for seniors. Since most Advantage plans are set up as managed-care systems (like an HMO or PPO), they generally require or encourage patients to seek treatment from a narrower range of providers. Unlike original Medicare, an Advantage plan might require that a patient receive a referral from a primary care physician before seeing a specialist. Alternatively, it may charge the patient more for seeing a provider who is not part of the insurer's network. These limits on access are intended to ensure that a patient's health status is more centrally monitored and that costly procedures are only ordered when they're medically necessary.

Extra Benefits

In exchange for giving up broader access to providers, members of Medicare Advantage plans typically receive benefits beyond what's available in original Medicare. Often these extra benefits involve coverage of treatment or services that are usually not part of original Medicare, such as routine eye exams, hearing exams, routine dental care, telehealth, over-the-counter (OTC) healthcare item and/or gym memberships. Often, a Medicare Advantage plan will cover the same things as original Medicare but require lower cost-sharing amounts (i.e., deductibles, coinsurance, and copayments) from patients.

Out-of-Pocket Limits

Finally, Medicare Advantage plans have an annual maximum out-of-pocket cost limit. This means once members reach this limit for hospital (Part A) and outpatient medical (Part B) covered services, they pay nothing and the plan pays 100% for covered services the rest of the year. Original Medicare doesn't have an annual out-of-pocket maximum limit.

Medicare Advantage vs. Original Medicare

Medicare Advantage plans generally must provide at least the same benefits as original Medicare, with a few exceptions. Whereas original Medicare might require patients to pay a 20% coinsurance amount to see a physician, a Medicare Advantage plan might require a flat $10 copayment for each visit. There might also be differences in the deductibles (often Medicare Advantage plans have no deductible) and the number of times a patient's care will be covered for a particular kind of treatment.

Although some aspects of a Medicare Advantage plan may require higher out-of-pocket expenses than original Medicare, the overall benefit package from the private plan must be at least as good as what's available from the government. Often Medicare Advantage plans provide lower out-of-pocket costs to enrollees.

One area where Advantage plans don't need to match original Medicare is hospice care. Since Advantage plans aren't required to cover hospice services, terminally ill individuals in Medicare Advantage remain insured for hospice care by the government through Medicare Part A.
data.markdown:
# Medicare Part C (Medicare Advantage)
Up until now, the information in this level has pertained to what we can call "original Medicare," which is administered by the federal government. At the time this material was being written, roughly half of seniors in the Medicare system were enrolled in original Medicare and had the coverage we've previously described.

The remaining half of Medicare's seniors were enrolled in "Medicare Advantage." Medicare Advantage is similar to original Medicare but is administered by private insurance companies that receive federal funding. This semi-privatized version of Medicare is sometimes known as "Medicare Part C" and was preceded by a similar program called "Medicare+Choice."

## Like an HMO or PPO
Medicare Advantage was created under the assumption that paying private insurers to administer Medicare benefits would save the government money and improve services for seniors. Since most Advantage plans are set up as managed-care systems (like an HMO or PPO), they generally require or encourage patients to seek treatment from a narrower range of providers. Unlike original Medicare, an Advantage plan might require that a patient receive a referral from a primary care physician before seeing a specialist. Alternatively, it may charge the patient more for seeing a provider who is not part of the insurer's network. These limits on access are intended to ensure that a patient's health status is more centrally monitored and that costly procedures are only ordered when they're medically necessary.

## Extra Benefits
In exchange for giving up broader access to providers, members of Medicare Advantage plans typically receive benefits beyond what's available in original Medicare. Often these extra benefits involve coverage of treatment or services that are usually not part of original Medicare, such as routine eye exams, hearing exams, routine dental care, telehealth, over-the-counter (OTC) healthcare item and/or gym memberships. Often, a Medicare Advantage plan will cover the same things as original Medicare but require lower cost-sharing amounts (i.e., deductibles, coinsurance, and copayments) from patients.

## Out-of-Pocket Limits
Finally, Medicare Advantage plans have an annual maximum out-of-pocket cost limit. This means once members reach this limit for hospital (Part A) and outpatient medical (Part B) covered services, they pay nothing and the plan pays 100% for covered services the rest of the year. Original Medicare doesn't have an annual out-of-pocket maximum limit.

## Medicare Advantage vs. Original Medicare
Medicare Advantage plans generally must provide at least the same benefits as original Medicare, with a few exceptions. Whereas original Medicare might require patients to pay a 20% coinsurance amount to see a physician, a Medicare Advantage plan might require a flat $10 copayment for each visit. There might also be differences in the deductibles (often Medicare Advantage plans have no deductible) and the number of times a patient's care will be covered for a particular kind of treatment.

Although some aspects of a Medicare Advantage plan may require higher out-of-pocket expenses than original Medicare, the overall benefit package from the private plan must be at least as good as what's available from the government. Often Medicare Advantage plans provide lower out-of-pocket costs to enrollees.

One area where Advantage plans don't need to match original Medicare is hospice care. Since Advantage plans aren't required to cover hospice services, terminally ill individuals in Medicare Advantage remain insured for hospice care by the government through Medicare Part A.

data.pageTitle: Medicare Part C: Eligibility and Premiums
data.html:
Medicare Part C: Eligibility and Premiums

To be eligible for enrollment in a Medicare Advantage plan, people must first enroll in Part A and Part B. Second, they must select a Medicare Advantage plan that is available in their state (based on permanent residence).

If someone enrolls in Medicare Advantage and is receiving Social Security benefits, premiums for Part B will still be deducted from the person's checks. The government will then use the money to compensate private insurers for administering Medicare on its behalf. Government payments to participating insurers are based on many factors, such as the number of enrollees in a plan and the quality of service compared to other plans in the area.

In addition to payments received from the federal government, Medicare Advantage plans can be funded through premiums from customers. However, a significant number of Advantage plans don't charge a premium beyond the usual Part B monthly premium.

data.pageTitle: Medicare Part C: Types of Advantage Plans
data.html:
Medicare Part C: Types of Advantage Plans

There are many different kinds of Medicare Advantage plans to choose from. Some of the most common varieties are HMOs, PPOs, PFFSs, MSAs, and SNPs.

HMO

An HMO (or "health maintenance organization") pays when patients see doctors who are part of the HMO's network. Treatment by providers who are not in the network is usually only covered in emergency situations. Care is received first from a "primary care physician," who then can decide whether treatment from a specialist is necessary. Care from a specialist will not be covered unless the primary care physician has issued a referral.

PPO

A PPO (or "preferred provider organization") has its own network of doctors but will still provide some coverage when patients see out-of-network physicians. Someone who has a PPO plan doesn't need a primary care physician and doesn't need a referral to see specialists.

PFFS

A PFFS (or "private fee-for-service plan") covers patients whenever they see a physician who agrees to accept their insurance. In general, providers can choose to accept a patient's PFFS insurance on a visit-by-visit basis. Being in a PFFS plan is somewhat similar to being in original Medicare and dealing with non-participating providers. In fact, original Medicare is a fee-for-service plan that is public rather than private.

MSA

An MSA (or "medical savings account") is very similar to a health savings account. MSAs are high-deductible plans in which the insurance company deposits money in a bank account for the patient's use. The patient can then use the money to pay for medical services. When the money is used to pay for care that would be covered by Medicare Part A or Part B, the cost is applied to the plan's deductible. If the money is used for non-Medicare services, the patient will usually face some tax-related consequences. If the account balance is not enough to cover all Medicare services for the year, the patient will pay out of pocket until the plan deductible has been satisfied. Once the deductible has been met, the insurance company will pay for the patient's care.

SNP

Some seniors are eligible for Medicare Advantage as part of an SNP (or "special needs plan.") An SNP is similar to an HMO plan, but it's usually only open to people in long-term care facilities, people enrolled in Medicaid and people with chronic, debilitating diseases, such as end-stage renal disease or AIDS.

data.pageTitle: Medicare Part C: Pros and Cons
data.html:
Medicare Part C: Pros and Cons

Choosing between original Medicare and a Medicare Advantage plan is one of the most important decisions a senior will have to make. Advantage plans are particularly popular among relatively young Medicare recipients who don't have many health issues. Here are some of the reasons why:

  • A Medicare Advantage plan might cover items or treatments that original Medicare won't. Examples often include eyeglasses, hearing aids, dental care, and gym memberships.

  • Medicare Advantage plans often require copayments for each office visit. These fees may be smaller, or at least more predictable, than the percentage-based coinsurance fees in original Medicare.

  • Medicare Advantage plans must have a cap on a patient's out-of-pocket expenses.

  • Since many Advantage plans cover prescription drugs, patients often don't need to purchase a separate Part D plan for their medications. (You'll read more about the Part D program in a later section.)

  • Depending on the plan and the services provided, deductibles in Medicare Advantage might be lower than in original Medicare.

  • Someone enrolled in Medicare Advantage might pay lower premiums than someone in original Medicare who tries to obtain similar coverage.

Conversely, here are some reasons why original Medicare might be preferred:

  • Many Medicare Advantage plans require that patients see doctors who are in the insurer's network. This can be a problem if a patient spends time in multiple parts of the country or simply wants more choices.

  • Insurers with Medicare Advantage plans may decide to leave the market. This is a common concern whenever federal subsidies to Advantage plans are changed.

  • Medicare Advantage patients in HMOs need referrals before they can see specialists.

  • People in Medicare Advantage plans generally don't have access to Medigap policies that could act as supplemental insurance.

  • Since there's very little standardization in Medicare Advantage, consumers may have a hard time comparing available plans.

  • Although premiums might be lower in Medicare Advantage, seriously ill patients might have lower medical bills overall if they have original Medicare and a Medicare supplement/Medigap policy. Cost differentials will depend on the specifics of an Advantage plan and the kind of needed care.

data.html:
Medicare Part C: Enrollment Periods

Like original Medicare, Medicare Advantage plans don't allow year-round enrollment. If someone wants to join a plan or wants to drop coverage and return to original Medicare, certain deadlines must be met.

The initial enrollment opportunity for someone who wants to join an Advantage plan occurs during the seven-month period around the person's 65th birthday or the person's 24th month of receiving disability payments from Social Security. For people who already have Part A but are signing up for Part B between January and March as late enrollees, enrollment in Medicare Advantage can be done from April 1 through June 30. (Remember, in order to join Medicare Advantage, the person must be enrolled in Part B.)

Changes During Enrollment Periods

From October 15 through December 7, insured individuals can make changes related to Medicare Advantage for the coming year. During this annual enrollment period, the following actions can be taken:

  • An insured can switch from one Medicare Advantage plan to another.

  • An insured can switch from Medicare Advantage back to original Medicare.

  • An insured can switch from original Medicare to Medicare Advantage.

Prohibited Changes

People in Medicare Advantage also have the chance to switch back to original Medicare or to another Medicare Advantage plan from January 1 through March 31. However, certain actions cannot be taken during this enrollment period:

  • An insured cannot switch from original Medicare to Medicare Advantage.

  • An insured cannot switch from an MSA plan back to original Medicare.

Special Enrollment Opportunities

There are many other deadlines related to Medicare Advantage for people in relatively specific situations. We won't go into detail about them here, but you should know they exist. Here are some events that might entitle someone to a special enrollment opportunity regarding a Medicare Advantage plan:

  • Turning 65 while already on Medicare because of a disability

  • Moving to a location that isn't serviced by the person's existing insurance

  • Returning to the United States after living abroad

  • Entering, living in, or leaving a nursing home

  • Returning from prison

  • Losing Medicaid eligibility

  • Losing other coverage from employment

  • Losing coverage from another Medicare Advantage plan

  • Losing drug coverage that's as good as Medicare's drug coverage

  • Being on Medicare and Medicaid at the same time

  • Receiving incorrect information from a federal employee and having it affect an enrollment decision

# Chapter Three

data.html:
Medicare Part D

During its first 40 years of existence, Medicare barely paid for any prescription drugs. If seniors or disabled people wanted help with the cost of medications, their options were usually limited to buying a Medigap supplemental insurance policy or spending down their assets until they qualified for Medicaid.

Prescription Drug Coverage

In 2006, Medicare introduced Part D, which allows Medicare beneficiaries to receive prescription drug coverage from private insurance companies. Participating insurers need to meet federal standards regarding deductibles and other kinds of cost sharing, but premiums and other out-of-pocket expenses can differ greatly from plan to plan. Although each plan is generally allowed to choose the specific drugs it will pay for, all Part D plans need to cover the same broad categories of drugs.

People in original Medicare can obtain Part D benefits by joining a private plan that's specifically designed to cover medications. People in Medicare Advantage can either choose an Advantage plan that includes drug coverage or join another private plan that's specifically designed to cover medications.

Premiums and Eligibility

Premiums and eligibility rules for a Part D plan cannot differ on the basis of a person's age or health status. Premiums will be higher for people who earn a relatively high income, and can also cost more for people who declined it in the past. Premiums can be deducted from Social Security payments, taken directly out of a person's bank account, or billed separately to the insured.

Dual Eligibles

Part D is optional for most people in Medicare, but there's an exception for "dual eligibles." A dual eligible is someone who is eligible for Medicare and Medicaid at the same time. Prescription drugs for dual eligibles will first be covered by Medicare Part D. Then, if a dual eligible's drugs are not covered by Part D, the cost of the person's prescriptions might be paid by Medicaid.

Even if Part D is selected, a few drugs will continue to be covered under other parts of Medicare. Drugs administered by a medical professional on an outpatient basis (such as chemotherapy drugs) will still generally be covered under Part B. Drugs provided to someone who has been admitted to a hospital will usually be covered under Part A.

data.pageTitle: Medicare Part D: Formularies and Tiers
data.html:
Medicare Part D: Formularies and Tiers

Before signing up for a Part D plan, someone who is eligible for prescription drug coverage should review the plan's "formulary." A formulary is a plan's list of covered drugs. Medicare Part D plans generally need to cover the kinds of drugs that a person with Medicare might need, but the specific drugs that are covered will be different in each plan. For example, although a plan might be required to cover at least some drugs that treat diabetes, it might not be required to cover all of them. (In the cases of a few diseases, such as cancer and HIV-AIDS, practically all drugs must be covered.) The list of covered drugs can change during the plan year, but enrollees usually must receive notice in advance.

Tiers

Most Part D plan formularies are tiered. A tier is a group of medications that are covered up to the same amount or at the same level. A plan typically has one tier made up of generic drugs (with the lowest copayments), a second tier of "preferred" brand-name drugs (with the same copayments but higher than the first tier), a third tier for other brand-name drugs, and a fourth tier of specialty drugs (with a highest copayment or coinsurance fee). Some plans may have five or six tiers in their formularies. It's important that Medicare beneficiaries considering enrollment in a particular Part D plan check the formulary to see if their medications are covered and, if so, at what coverage level. Occasionally, if a drug is medically necessary, members and their doctors may be able to request the plan to move a drug from one tier to a less expensive one.

Conditions on Use

Even if a drug is part of a formulary, the plan might put certain conditions on its use. Some drugs won't be covered unless the patient's doctor contacts the plan and provides authorization. For example, prescription opioids typically require prior authorization and may be subject to dosage and frequency limits. In a process known as "step therapy," a plan might also require that the patient try generic or less expensive drugs before it will cover more expensive brand ones. There can even be limits related to dosage. If step therapy or dosage limits are a health risk, the member's prescriber may contact the plan and request an exemption and coverage for the prescribed drug.

If a drug that a new plan member is currently taking isn't part of the plan's formulary, the plan must give the patient a one-time, 30-day transitional supply of it. While the member is using the supply, that person can work with a physician to either transition to a different drug or file an appeal.

data.html:
Medicare Part D: Cost-Saving Opportunities

Even with Part D prescription drug coverage, many Medicare beneficiaries are concerned that they can't afford the cost of their prescription drugs. For these people, several strategies can work to reduce their costs for prescription drugs and coverage.

They can:

  • Compare Medicare drug plans and choose the plan that provides the most coverage for medications currently prescribed.

  • Discuss with their doctors the list of medications they are currently taking to determine if some medications can be eliminated or brand-name drugs substituted with generic equivalent drugs.

  • Check to see if the manufacturer of an expensive medicine they take offers a Pharmaceutical Assistance program that would reduce their drug cost.

  • Check to see if their state offers a Pharmaceutical Assistance program that would help pay a portion of their Part D premium and/or cost-share (i.e., deductible, copay, or coinsurance).

  • See if they qualify for the "Extra Help" program sponsored by Medicare and Social Security. (You'll learn more about this option later.)

- Discuss with their doctors the list of medications they are currently taking to determine if some medications can be eliminated or brand-name drugs substituted with generic equivalent drugs.

data.pageTitle: Medicare Part D: Enrollment Periods and Late Penalties
data.html:
Medicare Part D: Enrollment Periods and Late Penalties

Even though it is optional, Part D should not be refused without careful consideration. As is the case with other parts of Medicare, enrollment is only allowed during specific timeframes every year or in special circumstances. And like Part B, Part D has a late enrollment penalty.

Extra Premiums

If people at first decline Medicare Part D coverage but want it at a later date, they might need to pay an extra 1% of premiums for each month that they delayed enrollment. Medicare calculates the 1% penalty by multiplying 1% of the "national base beneficiary premium" times the number of full, uncovered months that a person didn't have Part D or other drug coverage. The 1% penalty will be applied for as long as the person has Part D. The "national base beneficiary premium" that is part of this calculation tends to change each year.

Unlike Part B, Part D is only automatic for people who are dually eligible for Medicare and Medicaid. Even someone who is turning 65 and already receiving Social Security benefits won't be enrolled automatically. The times when Part D enrollment is allowed (including those times when a penalty might be enforced) are very similar to the enrollment periods for Medicare Advantage. In fact, you should keep in mind that many Medicare Advantage plans (if they cover drugs) are also part of Part D.

Initial Enrollment Period

The initial enrollment period for people turning 65 lasts for seven months and includes the three months prior to their birth month, the three months after their birth month and their actual birth month. Disabled people get their first chance to enroll during a period that lasts from their 22nd month of receiving Social Security payments through their 28th month.

People who declined Part B at first but decide to sign up for it during the annual enrollment period from January 1 through March 31 will also have a chance to enroll in Part D from April 1 through June 30 of that year.

Allowable Changes

From October 15 through December 7, insured individuals can make changes related to their drug coverage for the coming year. Allowable changes during this period are listed next:

  • An insured can switch from a Medicare Advantage plan without drug coverage to a Medicare Advantage plan with drug coverage.

  • An insured can switch from a Medicare Advantage plan with drug coverage to a Medicare Advantage plan without drug coverage.

  • An insured can join a Part D plan outside of Medicare Advantage.

  • An insured can switch from a Part D plan outside of Medicare Advantage to another Part D plan outside of Medicare Advantage.

  • An insured can drop drug coverage entirely.

From January 1 through March 31, a person can switch from a Medicare Advantage plan (with or without drug coverage) to original Medicare and enroll in a Part D plan outside of Medicare Advantage.

# Medicare Part D: Enrollment Periods and Late Penalties
Even though it is optional, Part D should not be refused without careful consideration. As is the case with other parts of Medicare, enrollment is only allowed during specific timeframes every year or in special circumstances. And like Part B, Part D has a late enrollment penalty.

## Extra Premiums
If people at first decline Medicare Part D coverage but want it at a later date, they might need to pay an extra 1% of premiums for each month that they delayed enrollment. Medicare calculates the 1% penalty by multiplying 1% of the "national base beneficiary premium" times the number of full, uncovered months that a person didn't have Part D or other drug coverage. The 1% penalty will be applied for as long as the person has Part D. The "national base beneficiary premium" that is part of this calculation tends to change each year.

Unlike Part B, Part D is only automatic for people who are dually eligible for Medicare and Medicaid. Even someone who is turning 65 and already receiving Social Security benefits won't be enrolled automatically. The times when Part D enrollment is allowed (including those times when a penalty might be enforced) are very similar to the enrollment periods for Medicare Advantage. In fact, you should keep in mind that many Medicare Advantage plans (if they cover drugs) are also part of Part D.

## Initial Enrollment Period
The initial enrollment period for people turning 65 lasts for seven months and includes the three months prior to their birth month, the three months after their birth month and their actual birth month. Disabled people get their first chance to enroll during a period that lasts from their 22nd month of receiving Social Security payments through their 28th month.

People who declined Part B at first but decide to sign up for it during the annual enrollment period from January 1 through March 31 will also have a chance to enroll in Part D from April 1 through June 30 of that year.

## Allowable Changes
From October 15 through December 7, insured individuals can make changes related to their drug coverage for the coming year. Allowable changes during this period are listed next:

- An insured can switch from a Medicare Advantage plan without drug coverage to a Medicare Advantage plan with drug coverage.
- An insured can switch from a Medicare Advantage plan with drug coverage to a Medicare Advantage plan without drug coverage.
- An insured can join a Part D plan outside of Medicare Advantage.
- An insured can switch from a Part D plan outside of Medicare Advantage to another Part D plan outside of Medicare Advantage.
- An insured can drop drug coverage entirely.

From January 1 through March 31, a person can switch from a Medicare Advantage plan (with or without drug coverage) to original Medicare and enroll in a Part D plan outside of Medicare Advantage.

data.html:
Other Enrollment Periods

Other enrollment periods are allowed when people find themselves in special circumstances. Here are several events that can trigger a temporary enrollment opportunity for Part D (possibly with a penalty for late enrollment):

  • Turning 65 while already on Medicare because of a disability

  • Moving to a location that isn't served by the person's current insurance

  • Returning to the United States after living abroad

  • Entering, living in, or leaving a nursing home

  • Returning from prison

  • Losing Medicaid eligibility

  • Losing other coverage from employment

  • Losing coverage from another Medicare Advantage plan

  • Losing drug coverage that's as good as Medicare's drug coverage

  • Being on Medicare and Medicaid at the same time

  • Receiving incorrect information from a federal employee and having it affect an enrollment decision

data.html:
Paying for Non-Covered Services

Despite Medicare's popularity, it should be clear to you that the program won't eliminate all medical expenses for the people in it. Millions of enrollees have recognized the gaps in coverage and tried to fill some of them by purchasing other insurance. Insurance products that can be used to complement the Medicare system include Medigap insurance policies, long-term care insurance policies, and group health insurance from a current or former employer. In the next several sections, we'll explore these options and go over how they address holes in the Medicare system.

data.html:
Medicare Supplements/Medigap Insurance

Seniors who want protection from the cost-sharing requirements in original Medicare can purchase a Medicare supplement from a private insurer. In some contexts, Medicare supplements are known as "Medigap" policies. Medicare supplements help pay for deductibles, coinsurance fees, and copayments that are associated with Medicare Part A and Medicare Part B. They cannot be used as a supplement to Medicare Advantage.

Medicare supplements are usually referred to by a particular letter (Plan A, Plan B, etc.) Since these plans are standardized by the federal government, all consumers who have the same lettered plan are entitled to the same basic benefits. The premiums, not the coverage, will vary across all insurers offering the same lettered plan.

Standardized Supplement Plans

As of 2024, the eight standardized Medicare supplement plans and their corresponding mandatory benefits are as follows:

  • Plan A: This plan covers all hospital copayments for Medicare Part A and pays for an additional year of hospitalization if the insured has used up his or her lifetime reserve days. It also covers most coinsurance fees or copayments that are required in Part B and for the first three pints of blood needed during hospitalization. If a patient is in hospice care, copayments for prescriptions and respite services are covered, too. All insurers in the Medigap market must sell Plan A.

  • Plan B: In addition to the benefits available through Plan A, this plan covers a patient's Part A deductible.

  • Plan D: In addition to the benefits available through Plan B, this plan includes the previously mentioned foreign travel benefits if a person needs care in another country. It also pays the patient's portion of days 21 through 100 at a skilled nursing facility.

  • Plan G: In addition to the benefits available through Plan D, this plan covers amounts charged by a non-participating provider that are in excess of Medicare's assigned charge.

  • Plan K: This plan covers hospitalization copayments and provides an extra year of hospitalization coverage for people who use up their lifetime reserve days. It also covers 50% of (1) Part B copayments and coinsurance fees; (2) a patient's first three pints of blood; (3) hospice coinsurance and copayments; (4) skilled nursing care for a limited number of days; and (5) the Medicare Part A deductible. When a person's annual out-of-pocket expenses for services in Plan K reach a certain amount, the plan will pay for 100% of covered care.

  • Plan L: Plan L covers the same things as Plan K, but it pays for 75% of most covered care instead of 50%. When a person's annual out-of-pocket expenses for services in Plan L reach a certain amount, the plan will pay for 100% of covered care.

  • Plan M: Plan M includes almost the same benefits as Plan D, but it only covers half of the Part A deductible.

  • Plan N: Plan N includes almost the same benefits as Plan D, but it makes the person responsible for copayments related to office visits and emergency rooms.

You probably noticed that the preceding list of Medicare supplement plans skipped a few letters. Plans C, E, F, H, I, and J were offered years ago but are no longer available to insurance applicants. Seniors who had already purchased one of these plans had the option of keeping it or switching to a plan on the list.

In some states, Medigap insurance can be obtained through a Medicare SELECT plan. A Medicare SELECT plan is a Medigap plan with its own network of providers. It's similar to an HMO plan and is often less expensive than a regular Medigap plan.

data.html:
Medigap Enrollment Periods

If they don't apply for a Medicare supplement on time, seniors may be subjected to medical underwriting. In other words, the insurance company might be allowed to charge people more because of their health or refuse to issue a policy at all.

Charging more or denying insurance on the basis of health isn't allowed if a senior applies for a supplement within six months after enrolling in Medicare Part B. However, someone who meets this deadline might still be faced with temporary exclusions for pre-existing conditions. If a senior was treated for a medical condition within six months prior to applying for Medigap insurance, the insurance company can sometimes refuse to pay for treatment of that specific condition until another six months has passed. The temporary exclusion can be shortened or eliminated if the senior was covered by other insurance within 62 days prior to applying for the policy. For the waiting period for pre-existing conditions to be entirely eliminated, the senior needs to have been covered by the other insurance for at least six months.

Regardless of when Medigap insurance is issued, the premium cost to the senior will be different from company to company. Some insurance companies base the cost on how old the senior was when the policy was first issued. Other companies base the cost on the enrollee's current age, and the premiums automatically increase as the senior grows older (age attained rating). (Remember, charging someone more because of age is different from charging someone more because of health.) Community rating is a third (and most common) method insurers may use to set premiums. Before enrolling, seniors should beware of age-attained rating and understand from the insurer how much costs may increase as they age.

data.html:
Long-Term Care Insurance

Very early in this course, you learned that care received at a nursing home is only covered by Medicare if several conditions (such as prior hospitalization) have been met. You also learned Medicare will not pay any bills for at-home care if a person only requires "custodial care." Custodial care is care that does not need to be performed or supervised by a skilled medical professional. It includes help with such basic activities as bathing, eating or getting dressed.

Long-term care insurance can be used to help pay for custodial care and other assistance that isn't covered by Medicare or other health insurance. Care that is considered "long-term" usually lasts at least 90 days and can be for an elderly person, a chronically ill person or someone who is recovering from a serious accident or disease.

For decades, long-term care insurance was considered something that was only used by people in nursing homes. Today's policies can cover people in a wide variety of settings. People can be insured for care that's provided at a nursing home, an assisted-living facility, or even in their own home.

Activities of Daily Living

To receive benefits through a long-term care policy, a person usually must either have a cognitive impairment (such as Alzheimer's disease) or need help with multiple "activities of daily living." Common activities of daily living are listed below, although the insurance company might have a longer list:

  • Bathing (including being able to move in or out of a shower or tub, clean oneself, and dry oneself)

  • Dressing (including being able to put on clothing and medical accessories, such as leg braces)

  • Eating (including being able to chew and swallow food and use utensils)

  • Transferring (including being able to move in and out of beds, cars, and chairs)

  • Toileting (including being able to get to a restroom and perform related personal hygiene)

  • Continence (including being able to control the bladder and bowels and perform related personal hygiene)

Long-term care insurance can be expensive, particularly for people who wait to buy it until they're older. But since Medicare's coverage of long-term care is so limited, it's often the only realistic option for seniors who know they might need care and don't want to eventually rely on Medicaid. Although Medicaid is likely to pay for long-term care, it is only available to people with little or no income or assets.

data.html:
Group Health Insurance

With more and more seniors continuing to work beyond their 65th birthday, the relationship between Medicare and employer-provided group insurance has become an increasingly important issue. Seniors with private group health insurance can usually put off their Medicare enrollment and avoid paying penalties, but that doesn't mean they'll be adequately insured.

Before making any decisions regarding Medicare enrollment, people who are insured through a private group health plan should contact a plan representative and ask how the plan deals with people who are eligible for Medicare. At the very least, answers to the following questions should be obtained:

  • Will the plan continue to provide the same level of coverage to someone who is eligible for Medicare? (A plan might reduce coverage for people who are 65 or older, even if they continue to work.)

  • Are retirees allowed to remain in the plan (either on their own or as the spouse of a current employee), and are they provided the same level of coverage as current employees? (Many plans don't cover former employees who have retired.)

  • If Medicare-eligible employees remain in the plan, will the plan be their primary insurer or their secondary insurer? (Primary insurance is billed first. Secondary insurance usually only pays for care that isn't covered by primary insurance.)

  • If a Medicare-eligible person chooses not to enroll in Medicare, will the group plan still cover treatment and services that would have normally been covered by Medicare? (Seniors might not be covered in this scenario if the group plan is intended to be secondary insurance.)

  • If Medicare-eligible members are allowed to remain in the plan, is the plan's prescription drug coverage considered "creditable coverage" in regard to Medicare Part D? (Having creditable coverage allows seniors to delay enrollment in Part D without paying penalties.)

  • If a Medicare-eligible person's family members are insured by the plan, will they lose their coverage if the person leaves the plan? (If someone insures his or her family through the same plan and then leaves the plan, the entire family might lose their insurance.)

Answers to those questions are especially important for people whose insurance is from an employer with fewer than 20 employees. By law, plans covering these people are usually considered secondary to Medicare. If a plan is secondary to Medicare, it might only cover Medicare-eligible members for things that Medicare isn't supposed to pay for.

Plans for companies with 20 or more employees cannot offer less coverage to a senior who is still working and can continue to be the person's primary insurance. Rules may be different for retirees and the disabled.

data.html:
Help With Premiums

Individuals with low incomes may be eligible for help with their Medicare premiums. Depending on their financial situation, they might even be able to have their deductibles, copayments, and coinsurance fees eliminated or greatly reduced.

The federal government provides low-income subsidies for millions of seniors in Medicare Part D. Part D subsidies for the poor are commonly referred to as "Extra Help." To be eligible for Extra Help, an applicant must live in one of the 50 states or the District of Columbia and must not have assets or income that total more than a certain amount. The figures are adjusted periodically to reflect changes in the federal poverty line.

Some valuable items, such as a person's home, car, and life insurance policies, are not counted toward the asset requirements for Extra Help. Higher incomes are allowed for the following individuals:

  • People who reside in Alaska or Hawaii

  • People who have dependents living with them

  • People who continue to work

Unless they're already on Medicaid or receiving benefits from Social Security because of their low income, Medicare beneficiaries usually won't receive Extra Help unless they actively apply for it. Government officials have estimated that as many as one-quarter of Part D recipients are eligible for this financial assistance.

A low-income person might also qualify for help from a Medicare Savings Program (MSP). Whereas Extra Help provides subsidies related to Part D, MSPs are meant to cover costs associated with Part A or Part B. The extent of the assistance and the rules regarding eligibility differ by state.

data.html:
Funding the System

Medicare's financial future has been a major concern for several years. Although it would be inappropriate for us to make any predictions here regarding the program's sustainability, you won't be able to fully understand Medicare unless you know how it's paid for.

Money for Part A comes mainly from payroll taxes that are paid by employees, employers, and people who work for themselves. At the time this course was being written, employees paid 1.45% of their income to fund Part A, and their employers were responsible for an additional 1.45%. People who are self-employed were responsible for the entire 2.9% tax.

In addition to withholding Medicare tax at 1.45%, employers needed to withhold a 0.9% additional Medicare tax from wages paid to an employee in excess of $200,000 in a calendar year. Additional Medicare Tax is only imposed on the employee. Taxpayers at that level also paid a 3.8% Medicare tax on investment income (not counting income from retirement accounts.)

Parts B and D of Medicare are paid for with general government funds and premiums from enrollees. Money for Part C comes from a combination of sources that are also used to fund the other parts of Medicare. If money that's intended to be spent on Medicare isn't needed to pay the program's current expenses, it's deposited into a trust fund.

Medicare's financial future has been a major concern for several years. Although it would be inappropriate for us to make any predictions here regarding the program's sustainability, you won't be able to fully understand Medicare unless you know how it's paid for.

Money for Part A comes mainly from payroll taxes that are paid by employees, employers, and people who work for themselves. At the time this course was being written, employees paid 1.45% of their income to fund Part A, and their employers were responsible for an additional 1.45%. People who are self-employed were responsible for the entire 2.9% tax.

In addition to withholding Medicare tax at 1.45%, employers needed to withhold a 0.9% additional Medicare tax from wages paid to an employee in excess of $200,000 in a calendar year. Additional Medicare Tax is only imposed on the employee. Taxpayers at that level also paid a 3.8% Medicare tax on investment income (not counting income from retirement accounts.)

Parts B and D of Medicare are paid for with general government funds and premiums from enrollees. Money for Part C comes from a combination of sources that are also used to fund the other parts of Medicare. If money that's intended to be spent on Medicare isn't needed to pay the program's current expenses, it's deposited into a trust fund.

data.html:
Medicare Fraud

Medicare beneficiaries can help the program save money by reporting fraud. If patients receive bills or statements for services they don't recognize, they should contact the provider to receive an explanation. If they still believe billed services were not actually provided, they should contact Medicare or the insurer that sponsors their Medicare Advantage plan. Providers who commit Medicare fraud can be fined several thousands of dollars and can face jail time.

Seniors should also be wary of unethical salespeople who might pretend to represent Medicare or try to sell them a bogus insurance product. Medicare doesn't engage in cold calling or door-to-door sales. Private companies offering Medicare Advantage plans, Part D plans, or Medigap plans cannot visit people's homes without being invited. They're also not allowed to engage in marketing activities over the phone unless the consumer initiates the contact.

Key Points

Let's review what we've covered in this level before the knowledge check.
data.markdown:
![image](https://d1u7daj727sadp.cloudfront.net/images/KeyPoints_INS.png)

# Key Points
Let's review what we've covered in this level before the knowledge check.

data.html:
Key Points

For roughly half a century, Medicare has played a leading role in the availability of quality health care for older Americans and the disabled. But for younger people who are either new to the program or years away from joining, the details can seem intimidating. Someone who will be relying on Medicare needs to know not only what's covered but also when to apply. The sooner you become familiar with how the system works, the easier it'll be to make smart insurance decisions.

This level has provided many details about insurance products and practices. Since we've addressed so much information, let's review some of this level's "key points." If you struggle to understand these key points, you should study the level again.

  • Medicare is a federal program, mainly for people who are senior citizens regardless of financial status.

  • Most Americans are eligible to join Medicare upon turning 65.

  • People of any age can join if they've been getting disability benefits from Social Security for two years or if they have end-stage renal disease.

  • Medicare Part A is hospital insurance that pays when someone is admitted to a hospital or skilled nursing facility. Part A has a deductible and various copayment requirements.

  • As long as you or your spouse has paid Medicare taxes for at least 10 years during your lifetime, you usually won't need to pay a premium for Part A.

  • Medicare Part A generally won't pay if you go to the emergency room but aren't admitted to the hospital.

  • In Medicare Part A, deductibles and copayments are reset at the start of each "benefit period."

  • A benefit period begins when a patient is admitted to a hospital or skilled nursing facility. The benefit period stops 60 days after the person's discharge.

  • There is no limit to the number of benefit periods you can have in your lifetime.

  • For a hospital stay, Part A will pay for the next 60 days of hospitalization after the deductible.

  • For days 61-90, Part A pays for most of it, but the patient pays a per-day copayment.

  • If a hospital stay is longer than 90 days, the patient will need to use a "lifetime reserve day." This will have a higher copayment.

  • You only get 60 reserve days for your entire life. If you use them up, you pay for everything until a new benefit period starts.

  • Nursing homes are only covered by Medicare if the patient is coming after a three-day hospital stay.

  • Nursing homes are only covered if they are "skilled nursing facilities" and the person needs "skilled care" from a trained medical professional, not just help with dressing, eating, bathing, etc.

  • Part A will pay for the first 20 days at a nursing home. For days 21-100, there's a daily copayment for the patient. There's no coverage after 100 days.

  • Medicare Part B is meant to pay for outpatient treatments and visits to doctors' offices. It also pays if you go to the hospital but aren't admitted.

  • Part B requires a monthly premium from all enrollees, even if you've paid Medicare taxes. A new premium is announced every year.

  • Deductibles for Part B are enforced on a yearly basis. After the deductible, patients pay a 20% coinsurance fee for all services.

  • Part B is optional. But if you wait to enroll, your premiums will be increased.

  • Medicare Part C (Medicare Advantage) is for people who opt to get their Medicare benefits from a private insurance company rather than from the government.

  • Medicare Advantage needs to at least cover the same things as Parts A and B. It sometimes covers extras (such as glasses and hearing aids) that aren't covered by regular Medicare.

  • Medicare Advantage might have lower deductibles, coinsurance fees, and copayments. But patients will need to see doctors within the insurer's network and will have other limitations imposed on their care.

  • If you get Medicare from the government, you can get help with coinsurance fees and copayments by buying a supplemental insurance policy (sometimes known as a "Medigap" policy).

  • Medigap policies aren't for people who have Medicare Advantage.

  • Medigap policies are sold by private insurers, but the government requires that they meet certain standards.

  • Each Medigap policy is known by a letter. A, B, C, D, etc. Each lettered policy is supposed to be essentially the same from insurer to insurer.

  • Medicare supplements can't cost more or be denied to people based on their health if they're purchased within six months of enrolling in Part B.

  • Even if someone enrolls on time, Medigap plans can exclude pre-existing conditions for 6 months (with a six-month look-back period).

  • If someone is still working but is eligible for Medicare, their group plan can continue to be their primary insurance IF the plan is for 20 people or more. They won't be penalized for enrolling late in Medicare.

  • If a senior is still working for an employer with a group plan for less than 20 people, Medicare might need to be the senior's primary insurance. The plan might only pay for things excluded by Medicare.

  • If you enroll in Medicare Advantage, you might have prescriptions already covered, or you might need to enroll in a Part D plan, too. It depends on your insurer.

  • Part D can't charge you more based on health but can charge you more based on income.

  • Although Part D is optional, late enrollees can be charged a penalty.
data.markdown:
# Key Points
For roughly half a century, Medicare has played a leading role in the availability of quality health care for older Americans and the disabled. But for younger people who are either new to the program or years away from joining, the details can seem intimidating. Someone who will be relying on Medicare needs to know not only what's covered but also when to apply. The sooner you become familiar with how the system works, the easier it'll be to make smart insurance decisions.

This level has provided many details about insurance products and practices. Since we've addressed so much information, let's review some of this level's "key points." If you struggle to understand these key points, you should study the level again.

- Medicare is a federal program, mainly for people who are senior citizens regardless of financial status.
- Most Americans are eligible to join Medicare upon turning 65.
- People of any age can join if they've been getting disability benefits from Social Security for two years or if they have end-stage renal disease.
- Medicare Part A is hospital insurance that pays when someone is admitted to a hospital or skilled nursing facility. Part A has a deductible and various copayment requirements.
- As long as you or your spouse has paid Medicare taxes for at least 10 years during your lifetime, you usually won't need to pay a premium for Part A.
- Medicare Part A generally won't pay if you go to the emergency room but aren't admitted to the hospital.
- In Medicare Part A, deductibles and copayments are reset at the start of each "benefit period."
- A benefit period begins when a patient is admitted to a hospital or skilled nursing facility. The benefit period stops 60 days after the person's discharge.
- There is no limit to the number of benefit periods you can have in your lifetime.
- For a hospital stay, Part A will pay for the next 60 days of hospitalization after the deductible.
- For days 61-90, Part A pays for most of it, but the patient pays a per-day copayment.
- If a hospital stay is longer than 90 days, the patient will need to use a "lifetime reserve day." This will have a higher copayment.
- You only get 60 reserve days for your entire life. If you use them up, you pay for everything until a new benefit period starts.
- Nursing homes are only covered by Medicare if the patient is coming after a three-day hospital stay.
- Nursing homes are only covered if they are "skilled nursing facilities" and the person needs "skilled care" from a trained medical professional, not just help with dressing, eating, bathing, etc.
- Part A will pay for the first 20 days at a nursing home. For days 21-100, there's a daily copayment for the patient. There's no coverage after 100 days.
- Medicare Part B is meant to pay for outpatient treatments and visits to doctors' offices. It also pays if you go to the hospital but aren't admitted.
- Part B requires a monthly premium from all enrollees, even if you've paid Medicare taxes. A new premium is announced every year.
- Deductibles for Part B are enforced on a yearly basis. After the deductible, patients pay a 20% coinsurance fee for all services.
- Part B is optional. But if you wait to enroll, your premiums will be increased.
- Medicare Part C (Medicare Advantage) is for people who opt to get their Medicare benefits from a private insurance company rather than from the government.
- Medicare Advantage needs to at least cover the same things as Parts A and B. It sometimes covers extras (such as glasses and hearing aids) that aren't covered by regular Medicare.
- Medicare Advantage might have lower deductibles, coinsurance fees, and copayments. But patients will need to see doctors within the insurer's network and will have other limitations imposed on their care.
- If you get Medicare from the government, you can get help with coinsurance fees and copayments by buying a supplemental insurance policy (sometimes known as a "Medigap" policy).
- Medigap policies aren't for people who have Medicare Advantage.
- Medigap policies are sold by private insurers, but the government requires that they meet certain standards.
- Each Medigap policy is known by a letter. A, B, C, D, etc. Each lettered policy is supposed to be essentially the same from insurer to insurer.
- Medicare supplements can't cost more or be denied to people based on their health if they're purchased within six months of enrolling in Part B.
- Even if someone enrolls on time, Medigap plans can exclude pre-existing conditions for 6 months (with a six-month look-back period).
- If someone is still working but is eligible for Medicare, their group plan can continue to be their primary insurance IF the plan is for 20 people or more. They won't be penalized for enrolling late in Medicare.
- If a senior is still working for an employer with a group plan for less than 20 people, Medicare might need to be the senior's primary insurance. The plan might only pay for things excluded by Medicare.
- If you enroll in Medicare Advantage, you might have prescriptions already covered, or you might need to enroll in a Part D plan, too. It depends on your insurer.
- Part D can't charge you more based on health but can charge you more based on income.
- Although Part D is optional, late enrollees can be charged a penalty.

# Chapter One

data.html:
Introduction

The practice of selling one's life insurance to strangers has its origins across the ocean in England, where economically poor individuals who suffered from serious illnesses could auction off their life insurance policies to the highest bidder at least as early as the 19th century. U.S. authorities who knew about these auctions and considered them despicable aimed to keep them out of our country by promoting non-forfeiture laws on a state level beginning in the 1860s.

Between that time and the 1980s, Americans with life insurance to their name were left in an odd position. As policy owners, they technically had the right to renounce policy benefits and put them in another person's hands. But beyond offering their policy as collateral to a creditor or surrendering it to the insurance company, they lacked formal ways of selling their policy for necessary cash.

Monopsony

When they look back on the state of life insurance as it was 40 years ago, multiple industry experts note that a person who wanted to sell an in-force yet unwanted policy usually had to deal with a "monopsony;" an environment in which people who market their goods and services can only do business with one buyer. That lone potential purchaser in those days was effectively the same company that issued the policy, and the "take it or leave it" offer from that buyer was never greater than the policy's cash surrender value.

Although the option of canceling a policy for its cash surrender value was certainly better than having no options at all, it was often far from a financial life saver for someone with a need to create immediate income from a policy. Then, as now, the cash surrender value often amounted to a very small amount if the owner had not yet paid significant premiums on the policy. At that time, insurance companies made no changes to surrender values for clients who had developed life-threatening illnesses.

Of course, the needy policyholder with a permanent life insurance policy also had the ability to receive a speedy delivery of dollars from the insurer by requesting a loan against the contract's cash value. But the amount available to the individual via a loan was sometimes very small compared to the policy's death benefit.
